initialTests.action: Interpret with local lab ranges. Reference anchors: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%. Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126. 2-hour 75-g OGTT: normal <140 mg/dL; prediabetes 140-199; diabetes >=200. Random plasma glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms supports diabetes diagnosis. UACR normal <30 mg/g; moderately increased 30-300; severely increased >300. Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.

treatmentOptions.action: Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.

reference_values: Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126.
Random plasma glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms supports diabetes diagnosis.
UACR normal <30 mg/g; moderately increased 30-300; severely increased >300.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
decision_steps: Diagnose with A1c >=6.5%, fasting plasma glucose >=126 mg/dL, 2-hour OGTT >=200 mg/dL, or random glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms; repeat confirmatory testing if asymptomatic.
At diagnosis, classify ASCVD, heart failure, CKD, obesity, hypoglycemia risk, medication access, and pregnancy possibility before selecting therapy.
Screen complications with eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, lipids, BP, foot neuropathy/perfusion exam, and eye/retinopathy follow-up.
If vomiting, dehydration, weight loss, ketones, acidosis, or very high glucose is present, route to DKA/HHS evaluation instead of routine outpatient titration.
Confirm that Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: A1c, fasting plasma glucose, or 2-hour OGTT to confirm; CMP/eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, fasting lipids; Ketones/anion gap if vomiting, weight loss, dehydration, or acute symptoms.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%. Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Begin individualized lifestyle, diabetes education, weight-management, BP/lipid, tobacco, vaccine, kidney, and cardiovascular risk-reduction plan.
Use metformin when appropriate and add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor preferentially when ASCVD, CKD, heart failure, obesity, or cardiorenal risk dominates.
Use insulin when catabolic symptoms, ketosis, severe symptomatic hyperglycemia, or A1c/glucose level suggests insulin deficiency or urgent control is needed.
Titrate therapy to individualized A1c and glucose goals while monitoring hypoglycemia, kidney function, adverse effects, and access.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

Any blurred vision, known retinopathy, eye injections/laser, or overdue dilated eye examination?
Any measured fever, rigors, hypothermia, or toxic appearance suggesting infection as a precipitant or mimic?

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: diagnostic frame from guideline-sourced endocrine workup
Key thresholds and interpretation caveats: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%. Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126.

Important mimics/exclusions: stress or steroid hyperglycemia, type 1/LADA versus type 2 diabetes, DKA/HHS versus uncomplicated hyperglycemia, hypoglycemia/drug effect, renal disease, infection, pregnancy, and secondary endocrine causes.
Diabetes-range A1c/FPG/OGTT changes diagnosis and treatment intensity.
UACR >=30 or reduced eGFR changes kidney-protective therapy.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.

root.description: Activate when structured or extractable context matches Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus triggers or the clinician selects workup type_2_diabetes_mellitus_v1.

children.action: Before choosing a non-emergency Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus branch, document: presenting trigger, onset/trajectory, focused exam, vital signs, threshold labs/results, medication/allergy context, comorbidities, demographics, pregnancy/applicability status, and current workup findings.

children.label: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: obtain concurrent diagnostic, severity, and treatment-safety data
children.edgeLabel: Selected Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus workup or documented findings: How much are you drinking and urinating, including polyuria, polydipsia, overnight urination, thirst intensity, access to water, and any dehydration or confusion?; How has weight changed from baseline, and was the change intentional or associated with appetite, fluid status, or systemic symptoms?; Any blurred vision, known retinopathy, eye injections/laser, or overdue dilated eye examination?; Any measured fever, rigors, hypothermia, or toxic appearance suggesting infection as a precipitant or mimic?; DKA/HHS symptoms, dehydration, vomiting, altered mental status.; Random glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms.; A1c, fasting plasma glucose, or 2-hour OGTT to confirm.

criteria.description: Obtain Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus diagnostic/severity/treatment-safety data together rather than as a one-step chain.

children.action: Obtain together: A1c, fasting plasma glucose, or 2-hour OGTT to confirm.; CMP/eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, fasting lipids.; Ketones/anion gap if vomiting, weight loss, dehydration, or acute symptoms.; Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.; Diabetes safety tests: review POC/plasma glucose trend, BMP with potassium/bicarbonate/anion gap and creatinine, beta-hydroxybutyrate or urine ketones when acutely ill, and UACR/lipids for complication-directed therapy.; A1c or plasma glucose criteria for diagnosis/classification, plus CGM/glucometer pattern when available.; CMP/eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, lipids, and ketones/anion gap/beta-hydroxybutyrate when acute symptoms or insulin deficiency are possible.; A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%.; Measure blood pressure; Measure heart rate; Measure current weight; Document mental status; normal <5.7%; 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%; normal <100 mg/dL; diabetes >=126; normal <5.7%; 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%.

children.action: DKA/HHS symptoms, dehydration, vomiting, altered mental status.; Random glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms.; Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.; DKA/HHS physiology, recurrent severe hypoglycemia, vomiting/dehydration, altered mental status, infected foot wound, or pregnancy with hyperglycemia.. Begin individualized lifestyle, diabetes education, weight-management, BP/lipid, tobacco, vaccine, kidney, and cardiovascular risk-reduction plan.; Use metformin when appropriate and add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor preferentially when ASCVD, CKD, heart failure, obesity, or cardiorenal risk dominates.; Use insulin when catabolic symptoms, ketosis, severe symptomatic hyperglycemia, or A1c/glucose level suggests insulin deficiency or urgent control is needed.; Titrate therapy to individualized A1c and glucose goals while monitoring hypoglycemia, kidney function, adverse effects, and access.; Diabetes-range A1c/FPG/OGTT changes diagnosis and treatment intensity.; UACR >=30 or reduced eGFR changes kidney-protective therapy.; Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.

children.label: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: initiate treatment/disposition for the active threshold-defined branch
children.edgeLabel: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus treatment/disposition indicated by: normal <5.7%; 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%; normal <100 mg/dL; diabetes >=126; normal <140 mg/dL; Begin individualized lifestyle, diabetes education, weight-management, BP/lipid, tobacco, vaccine, kidney, and cardiovascular risk-reduction plan.; Use metformin when appropriate and add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor preferentially when ASCVD, CKD, heart failure, obesity, or cardiorenal risk dominates.; Use insulin when catabolic symptoms, ketosis, severe symptomatic hyperglycemia, or A1c/glucose level suggests insulin deficiency or urgent control is needed.; Titrate therapy to individualized A1c and glucose goals while monitoring hypoglycemia, kidney function, adverse effects, and access.; Diabetes-range A1c/FPG/OGTT changes diagnosis and treatment intensity.; UACR >=30 or reduced eGFR changes kidney-protective therapy.; Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.

criteria_options.criteria_text: Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions. Interpret with local lab ranges. Reference anchors: A1c: normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; diabetes >=6.5%. Fasting plasma glucose: normal <100 mg/dL; prediabetes 100-125; diabetes >=126. 2-hour 75-g OGTT: normal <140 mg/dL; prediabetes 140-199; diabetes >=200. Random plasma glucose >=200 mg/dL with classic symptoms supports diabetes diagnosis. UACR normal <30 mg/g; moderately increased 30-300; severely increased >300. Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects. parallel_actions: Begin individualized lifestyle, diabetes education, weight-management, BP/lipid, tobacco, vaccine, kidney, and cardiovascular risk-reduction plan.
Use metformin when appropriate and add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor preferentially when ASCVD, CKD, heart failure, obesity, or cardiorenal risk dominates.
Use insulin when catabolic symptoms, ketosis, severe symptomatic hyperglycemia, or A1c/glucose level suggests insulin deficiency or urgent control is needed.
Titrate therapy to individualized A1c and glucose goals while monitoring hypoglycemia, kidney function, adverse effects, and access.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
Diabetes-range A1c/FPG/OGTT changes diagnosis and treatment intensity.
UACR >=30 or reduced eGFR changes kidney-protective therapy.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
